senses.definition: A bird of paradise from Australia or New Guinea, usually with shiny black and green feathers and a special courtship dance.
examples: We spotted a riflebird displaying its bright feathers in the rainforest.
The riflebird is known for its unusual mating dance.

senses.definition: Plural of riflebird: several birds of paradise from Australia or New Guinea, often seen in rainforest areas.
examples: The park is home to riflebirds and many other tropical species.
We heard the calls of riflebirds deep in the forest.

senses.definition: Having spiral grooves cut inside the barrel so that a bullet spins when it is fired.
examples: The hunter carried a rifled shotgun for better accuracy.
Modern military weapons usually have rifled barrels.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of rifle: searched something quickly and roughly, often to steal.
examples: Thieves rifled the office drawers before escaping.
She realized someone had rifled her handbag on the train.

senses.definition: All people or things connected with rifles, viewed as a group, often used humorously or creatively.
examples: The book is a classic in the world of rifledom.
He was a legend in local rifledom for his marksmanship.

senses.definition: A person who strongly supports or belongs to a rifle club, army rifle unit, or rifle movement, sometimes used historically or humorously.
examples: The old photograph shows a proud rifleite in uniform.
Rifleites gathered for the annual target competition.

senses.definition: A soldier who is trained to use a rifle, especially in the army.
examples: The rifleman took position on the hill.
He served as a rifleman during the war.

senses.definition: Skill in shooting with a rifle accurately and safely.
examples: Good riflemanship requires patience and steady hands.
The course focuses on basic riflemanship and safety.

senses.definition: Plural of rifleman: soldiers who are trained to use rifles.
examples: The riflemen advanced across the field.
Several riflemen were posted at the entrance.

senses.definition: Strong enough to stop bullets fired from a rifle.
examples: The vehicle was fitted with rifleproof glass.
They took cover behind a rifleproof wall.

senses.definition: Someone who rifles something, especially a thief who searches things quickly to steal from them.
examples: The rifler emptied every drawer in the office.
Police suspected a skilled rifler was behind the series of burglaries.

senses.definition: A tool with a curved, rough surface used for shaping or smoothing metal, wood, or stone.
examples: The sculptor used a rifler to refine the statue's details.
A small rifler is perfect for delicate metal work.

senses.definition: Plural of riflery: different activities or places connected with shooting rifles, especially for sport.
examples: Over the years he took part in various rifleries around the country.
The fairgrounds once hosted summer rifleries and competitions.

senses.definition: Plural of rifler: people who rifle through things, especially thieves.
examples: The riflers left the house in complete disorder.
Experienced riflers can clear out an office in minutes.

senses.definition: Plural of rifler when referring to small curved files used for detailed work on metal, wood, or stone.
examples: The jeweler kept several riflers on his workbench.
These riflers are ideal for carving fine patterns.

senses.definition: The sport or practice of shooting at targets with rifles.
examples: He took a riflery class at summer camp.
Riflery requires concentration and strict safety rules.

senses.definition: Plural of rifle: long guns that you hold against your shoulder to fire bullets.
examples: The soldiers carried rifles on their backs.
He collects old military rifles.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rifle: searches something quickly and roughly, often to steal or find something.
examples: He rifles the drawers looking for his passport.
The burglar rifles the desk for cash.

senses.definition: A special sight with lenses that is fixed on a rifle to help the shooter aim more accurately.
examples: He adjusted the riflescope before taking the shot.
A good riflescope can greatly improve accuracy.

senses.definition: A single shot fired from a rifle, or the sound of it.
examples: A loud rifleshot echoed through the valley.
The soldiers advanced after the first rifleshot.

senses.definition: Spiral cuts inside the barrel of a gun that make the bullet spin and fly straighter.
examples: The rifling in the barrel improves the rifle's accuracy.
Worn rifling can reduce the effectiveness of a weapon.

senses.definition: The act of searching through something quickly and roughly, especially to steal.
examples: The rifling of her drawers left the room in chaos.
There were clear signs of rifling in the office.

senses.definition: Present participle of rifle: searching something quickly and roughly, often to steal or find something.
examples: She stood there rifling through her bag for her keys.
Thieves were rifling the drawers when the alarm went off.

senses.definition: Plural of rifling: the spiral grooves inside several gun barrels.
examples: The riflings of the old rifles were badly worn.
Experts compared the riflings on the recovered weapons.

senses.definition: A nonsense or playful word with no fixed standard meaning, sometimes used humorously as a mild exclamation or invented name.
examples: He just muttered "riflip" and walked away.
The children named their game Riflip.

senses.definition: Plural or playful form of riflip, used as a nonsense word or for humorous effect.
examples: He ended the story with a cheerful "riflips".
They titled the comic "Riflips and Friends".

senses.definition: Plural of rif: short repeated musical phrases, especially in jazz, rock, or pop music.
examples: The guitarist played fast rifs throughout the song.
Catchy rifs make the track memorable.

senses.definition: A serious and often sudden split or disagreement between people or groups.
examples: The argument caused a rift between the two friends.
There is a growing rift within the political party.

senses.definition: A large crack or opening in the ground, rock, or ice.
examples: They carefully crossed the rift in the glacier.
A deep rift opened in the road after the earthquake.

senses.definition: To split or break apart, or to make something split or break apart, especially suddenly.
examples: A bolt of lightning rifted the dark sky.
The conflict rifted the once united group.

senses.definition: Obsolete spelling of rift, meaning to split or burst open.
examples: The ground did rifte beneath their feet.
The clouds did rifte to reveal the moon.

senses.definition: Split or cracked, often by natural forces or strong pressure.
examples: The rifted rocks formed a dramatic landscape.
Rifted ice sheets floated on the dark water.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of rift: split or broke apart.
examples: The conflict rifted the community in two.
Deep cracks rifted the dry earth.

senses.definition: Someone or something that causes a rift, especially a person who creates division between others.
examples: He was seen as a rifter in the otherwise united team.
Political rifters can damage long-term relationships between countries.

senses.definition: More full of cracks, splits, or gaps than something else.
examples: The ground became riftier as they climbed higher.
This part of the coastline is much riftier than the smooth southern beaches.

senses.definition: Most full of cracks, splits, or gaps compared to all others.
examples: It was the riftiest stretch of road they had ever seen.
The riftiest cliffs were also the most dangerous to climb.

senses.definition: The process of the Earth's crust splitting and pulling apart to form rifts.
examples: Continental rifting can eventually create new oceans.
The area shows clear signs of recent rifting.

senses.definition: Present participle of rift: splitting or breaking apart.
examples: The ground was rifting beneath their feet.
Disagreements were rifting the team into small groups.

senses.definition: Without any rifts, cracks, or serious divisions.
examples: The stone surface appeared completely riftless.
Their riftless friendship lasted a lifetime.

senses.definition: Plural of rift: serious splits or disagreements between people or groups.
examples: Old rifts within the party began to reopen.
Family rifts can last for generations.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rift: splits or breaks apart, or causes something to split or break apart.
examples: Tension rifts the group into two sides.
A mighty crack rifts the ancient wall.

senses.definition: Full of cracks, splits, or gaps.
examples: They walked carefully over the rifty ground.
Rifty cliffs lined the rough coastline.

senses.definition: A large structure or equipment used for a special purpose, such as drilling for oil or gas.
examples: The workers spent weeks on the offshore oil rig.
The drilling rig operates day and night.

senses.definition: The special equipment, clothes, or setup that someone uses, especially for a particular activity.
examples: He showed up in a full climbing rig.
Their camping rig included a tent, stove, and lantern.

senses.definition: To fix or arrange something in a quick, sometimes dishonest or unfair way.
examples: They tried to rig the election.
He rigged up a simple shelter from plastic sheets.

senses.definition: The capital city of Latvia, located on the Baltic Sea.
examples: They spent a weekend exploring Riga.
Riga is famous for its Art Nouveau buildings.

senses.definition: A playful or nonsense word, sometimes used to imitate a rhythmic sound or used in songs and rhymes.
examples: The children sang, adding a silly "rigadig" at the end of each line.
He knocked on the door with a little rigadig rhythm.

senses.definition: A lively old dance for couples, similar to a rigaudon.
examples: The musicians played a rigadon at the historical ball.
They learned a simple rigadon as part of the workshop.

senses.definition: A lively dance from the 17th and 18th centuries, or the music written for it.
examples: The court danced a rigadoon in the grand hall.
The piece ends with a playful rigadoon.

senses.definition: Plural of rigadoon: several lively dances from the 17th and 18th centuries or pieces of music for them.
examples: The concert included several Baroque rigadoons.
They practiced different rigadoons for the performance.

senses.definition: A playful or nonsense word for some kind of gadget, contraption, or complicated thing whose proper name you do not know or do not want to use.
examples: He fixed the door with some kind of metal rigamajig.
Pass me that plastic rigamajig next to the toolbox.

senses.definition: A long, confusing, or unnecessary process, story, or set of actions.
examples: Filling out the paperwork was a complete rigamarole.
He went through the whole rigamarole of explaining it again.

senses.definition: Plural of rigamarole: several long, confusing, or unnecessary processes or stories.
examples: I'm tired of all these rigamaroles at the bank.
Different offices had their own rigamaroles.

senses.definition: An extremely rare or obsolete word, probably a variant or error for "irrigation" meaning the supply of water to land or crops.
examples: The old text mentions "rigation" of the fields.
Scholars believe "rigation" here refers to watering the land.

senses.definition: Short, wide tubes of pasta with ridges on the outside.
examples: We had rigatoni with tomato sauce for dinner.
Rigatoni holds thick sauces very well.

senses.definition: Plural of rigatoni: pieces or dishes of pasta made as short, wide tubes with ridges.
examples: The rigatonis were cooked al dente.
She prepared two different rigatonis for the party.

senses.definition: A lively French dance for couples from the 17th century, or the music written for this dance.
examples: The orchestra played a graceful rigaudon.
They studied the basic steps of the rigaudon.

senses.definition: Plural of rigaudon: several lively French court dances or pieces written for them.
examples: The suite includes two elegant rigaudons.
They rehearsed different rigaudons for the ball.

senses.definition: An old dialect or regional word, probably meaning something that weakens or harms the body, but now extremely rare.
examples: In the old tale, poverty is called the poor man's rigbane.
They used the word rigbane for any wasting sickness.

senses.definition: A very bright blue-white star in the constellation Orion, also called Beta Orionis.
examples: On a clear night you can see Rigel shining in Orion.
Rigel is one of the brightest stars in the sky.

senses.definition: Relating to the star Rigel in the constellation Orion.
examples: Astronomers discussed Rigelian properties of the star system.
The story is set near a distant Rigelian world.

senses.definition: In science fiction, a person or creature imagined to come from the star Rigel.
examples: The hero meets a mysterious Rigelian on his journey.
Rigelians in the novel have advanced technology.

senses.definition: The state or process of becoming stiff or rigid.
examples: The rigescence of the muscles made movement painful.
Botanists studied the plant's rigescence in cold conditions.

senses.definition: Becoming stiff or rigid, or tending to become so.
examples: Rigescant tissues were noted during the examination.
In the cold wind, the leaves turned dry and rigescent.
